segments.text: And now here's one module at the news. Good evening. First year is the official weather forecast
from the United States Weather Bureau at the Billings Airport. For Billings and vicinity
cloudy with light to moderate snow and north-westerny winds 25 to 35 miles per hour, causing near
blizzard conditions at times early this evening. Snow ending late tonight or early tomorrow
morning, decreasing cut in this Tuesday afternoon. Colder tonight with a low of 18 continued quite
cool tomorrow with a high of 30, low tomorrow night, 12 above.
Regional forecast, snow will spread into northern and eastern Wyoming in the western
decoters early tonight, and into the eastern decoters by tomorrow morning accompanied by strong
north-western winds and reserve conditions at times.
Snow will end in north-western Montana early tonight, and over the most of the state Tuesday
4 noon and in western decoters and extreme northern Wyoming tomorrow afternoon.
will fall to 15 to 25 above in North Dakota, 25 to 35 in South Dakota, 20 to 30 in Wyoming
and 15 to 25 in Montana tonight, except 5 to 15 in North Eastern Montana.
From all of the rather cold in most sections with high temperatures ranging from 25 to 35
in South Dakota in Wyoming and west of the continent of the bride in Montana, to 15 to 25 in
North Dakota and North Eastern Montana.
The local side of the news this evening, the Yellowstone Board of County commissioners
today signed the resolution creating the fifth township in Yellowstone County.
The board action was taken following a hearing and a petition for the creation of a township
submitted by Custer Residence.
Custer has been in the Warden township, which Custer Residence believed too large to adequately
service the town of Custer.
The board reported no protests were registered against the petition.
Emerson Body, famous Negro minister and lecturer, spoke this morning before the student body
of Eastern Montana College of Education on human and race relations.
Dr. Body emphasized the fact that in world terms, the white race is in the minority, and
that the shrinking of the world through improved methods of communication and transportation
has forced man's attention to the necessity of believing in the theory of world brotherhood.
Dr. Body will speak before the public tonight at the College Auditorium at 8 p.m.
address will be directed to the subject of Christianity and freedom.
The current lectures are included in the 1950 series of lectures at Eastern.
Ten crew leaders of the Abelings District of the Bureau of Census will attend crew leading
training schools.
They first to be at the commercial club from March 8 through March 11 and the second to
be at Eastern Montana College of Education from March 13 through March 15.
The schools will be on a technical advisor ship of Mrs. Esther Cooper of the city.
will be assisted by William Long of Billings Irrigation Technician.
According to District Supervisor Thomas D. Kelly, these crew leading training schools
are being staged for all over the 40-year-old census districts in the continental United States.
The local chapter of the Order of Demolay was advised by the Grand Council at Edwin H. Keith.
And Harry L. Fine of Billings, former Demolay members, have been awarded the Legion of Honor degree.
for Keith, his treasurer of the Keith Autumn supply company, Mr.
Pine, his western manager of the Beacon Balls rubber footwear.
A Chevrolet degree, which is awarded to active demolay members for work in the local
chapter, has been given to Dale Strait, Thomas Larson,
Frederick Johnson, and Gilbert Reak.
Every year the Grand Council honors active and former demolays throughout the nation on
the basis of their service to mankind, work in a business or a profession, or work in community
welfare activities.
days. The names are submitted by delegates attending the annual conference of the Grand
Council. This year's conference was staged at Port Worth, Texas.
A moving man whose body was found Saturday near the highway south of Tostorne still has
not been identified this evening, shut George Kickburshire Broadway County says he is
sending a package to the Federal Bureau of Investigation in Washington for examination
by the FBI Scientific Crime Detection Laboratory. Items being sent to the package include
the bullet-rather, taken from the man's head, is quoting and his finger-pence.
Governor John W. Bonner says the special firm to help rehabilitate wayward Montana girls
has been set up through the generosity of a Midwest business man.
Mr. Bonner says the money was given to Mrs. Elizabeth Steve, director of the State Vocational
School for Girls and Helmer, by Charles Ward, president of the St. Paul, Minnesota firm.
gave Mrs. Steer $1,000 recently after her work to rehabilitate young girls came to his
attention.
Mrs. Steed established a Charles Ward fund with the money.
She says she will use it for three purposes.
One, to get her senior class graduated in June, to finance a business college education for
two rehabilitated girls, and to obtain marriage annulments for some underage girls in her
care.
Governor Bonan says he has extremely gratified that Mrs. Steed's work has received national
recognition for the man of Mr. Ward Stetcher. For consciousness entered the New York stock
market today and prices drifted in an air range as the market closed, several of the
coal strikes of course had been anticipated and discounted by the stock list last week
and there were no new incentives. The Dow Jones closing stock air reaches industrial
200-4 and 8800 sub-1700s, railroads 56 and 42-hundreds off 1800s, utilities 42 and 90-hundreds
off 1100s and 65 stocks, 74 and 44-hundreds off 500s. Sales today totaled 1,470,000 shares
against 1,510,000 shares traded last Friday. Weak call and soybean futures closed sharply
higher in trading at Chicago, oats and rye were irregular, wheat was up one and one quarter
to two cents, corn gained one eighth to one cent, oats were up one eighth to up one quarter,
rye was up one and half to up three quarters and soybeans were up one quarter to one man,
one quarter. Wheat was higher, but other grains were irregular at outside markets.
Well on the national land, international news scene this evening, this has been a crucial day
in the murder trial of Dr. Herman Sandrup, Manchester New Hampshire, his boyhood pal who became an
attorney general hounded him for an answer to the question, why did he inject air into
the veins of a woman cancer patient he's accused of slaying?
Dr. Sanders says his mind snapped.
He believed the patient was already dead.
It was just the appearance of her face and the remembrance of her long suffering that might
have touched me off and made me do something that doesn't make sense, he said.
I was obsessed to do it, but he insisted that he believed the patient was dead.
Sandy said, the very fact that she was dead gave me assurance I could do her no harm.
Under Fanny's questioning, Sanders said he had given the patient larger than usual doses
of drugs to ease her pain.
Fanny said, you knew overdoses might result in a fatality.
Dr. Sanders said overdose is merely a relative term, but the prosecutor said they can be
fatal.
Is that right?
That's right.
Sanders said adding, but I didn't consider this an overdose.
testified calmly. People in the courtroom lean forward to catch his testimony and the jurors
listened intently. Sandra completed his testimony in just under four hours and he finished
up by saying he had no intention of doing harm at any time to his cancer patient. Then
we stepped from the stand. Well, when you first call loads of coal are rolling out of the
minefields and Mr. John L. Lewis is squaring his debts this evening. While the miners were
on strike, CIO unions helped feed the hungry coal diggers and their families. The steel workers
Spitched in with a half a million dollars the auto workers showed up with food and other
relief assistance.
John L. didn't forget.
His troubles over Mr. Lewis moved quickly to the aid of the CIO auto workers who have been
on strike for eight weeks in Chrysler.
Lewis offered the auto men a one million dollar loan and called upon other great unions
to help out too.
The mining and chief intimated the auto workers and the UMW are allies in about the same
battle.
In the coal contract this few just suddenly said the UMW was opposed by a lied financial
interests associated in a major sense with the financial group that dominates the auto
industry.
But the biggest news around the fireside is that the coal is coming out of the mines
this evening.
Car loads a few already have been dug from the mines in West Virginia in Kentucky, and
a jackpot hall is coming in tonight.
Meanwhile employment is up and unemployment is up.
That sounds bad, Mexico, but the fact is the number of unemployed in February jumped
to $4,684,000 the highest since August, 1941.
That's according to figures released by the Census Bureau today.
At the same time, employment in February rose 6,000 above the January total.
The striking co-miters were not taken into consideration.
The reason they seemingly contradictory figures jive is that the civilian labor force,
everyone looking for a job, continued to rise and reached $60,000.
information brought to you six nights each week at six-fifteen by the Midland National Bank,
an institution devoted to service for the people of the Midland Empire and the member of
the Federal Opposite Insurance Corporation. In a moment for latest news and weather, but first
here is Howard Harding with a message from the Midland National Bank.

And you have helped to pay those expenses too, or he wouldn't stay in long in business.

Yes, right there in your hand.
There couldn't possibly be a mark-up or a special deal or so-called goodbye, because a dollar

And now we take you to the U.S. Federal Office, the Billings Airport, for the latest
official weather information.
Good evening, this is Ray Cunning with The Weather Summary and Forecasts.
Last night and today a strong surge of Pacific air moved through Montana and into the western
encoders, and not in Wyoming, followed by colder air spreading in from the north.
This storm, which was centered in southwestern Montana at this time yesterday evening, is
now centered over the eastern decoders, and the strong winds with snow and blowing snow
and near blizzard conditions are now spreading into the western decoders.
In buildings, the snow is gradually decreasing, but blowing snow with 25 to 35 mile per hour
north-western winds, with near blizzard conditions at times, will be continuing during the early
part of the night, with a snow ending, and the wind decreasing during the latter part
of the night, and cloudiness decreasing tomorrow afternoon.
It will be colder tonight with the lowest temperature near 18 above.
Tomorrow, the high temperature will be near 30, and the last tomorrow night about 12 above.
high-end conditions for the operation of a light aircraft will not be favorable during
the early part of tomorrow for noon due to scattered snow showers and surface winds between
20 to 25 miles per hour. But during the afternoon the clouds will be breaking and the surface
winds will be decreasing for local operations. The highest wind here at the Billings Airport
occurred during the latter part of the forenoon and the wind reached north
westerly 47 miles per hour with gusts to 55 miles per hour. Most places east of
the continental divide in Montana experienced gusty winds today between 35 and
45 miles per hour or higher and near-blessed conditions. During the past 18
hours some beneficial amounts of precipitation were reported over Montana. Here
At the Bellings Airport, we receive 600-7-inch Lewis Town, reported 1800s, Calis Bell
57-100s, Cutbank 700s, Great Falls 7-600s, Helena 32-100s, Boseman 28-100s, and Haber 85-100s.
Our high temperature today was 46 miles, 30-30-36, shared in 58, Livingston 35, and Cody 38.
Here again is the forecast for bellings and vicinity.
Cloudy, with light snow at times and blowing snow,
with the north-westernly winds 25 to 35 miles per hour,
causing near-blizzard conditions at times early tonight,
with the snow ending late tonight,
and the cloudiness decreasing tomorrow afternoon.
It will be colder tonight with the lowest 18,
and continued quite cold tomorrow, the higher on 30.
The load tomorrow night will be 12 above.
That's all from the weather bureau for now.
Here again is your announcer.
Our next weather broad jazz will be heard at 11 o'clock this evening.
And now here's Howard Harding and Venews.
The big coal strike just ended has overshadowed the strike
at Plants of the Chrysler Motor Company.
That strike is now seven weeks old.
And John A. Lewis has just come out with an offer of a $1 million known
to aid the CIO auto workers in the Chrysler Strike.
Lewis made the offer to the Outer Workers President Walter Ruther in a letter saying that
the policy committee of the United Mine Workers had authorized.
Lewis said that the Mine Workers Union hopes that the other unions in the CIO and the
FABL will extend similar aid.
Lewis's offer is seen as a response to the gift check for $500,000 which CIO President
Philip Murray sent to the Mine Workers in their contract flight.
In offering a loan of $1 million to the CIO auto union, John L. Lewis emphasized the
current crisis strike, but he made it clear that the mine union authorized the loan for
the overall auto union drive.
Lewis mentioned only Chrysler by name, but he implied that the loan could be used to
help defray expenses of the coming contract talks with General Motors.
Meanwhile the back-to-work parade of the nation's coal mines is picking up fast.
One coal state after another reports that the miners are flocking back to the jobs.
back to back back to work movement got off to a slow start this morning with only
about thirty thousand miners on hand for the early shifts. Good many of the
diggers waited until they got the official word from Mr. John L. Lewis at
the traditional union meetings. Nearly all of the miners are expected to be
digging coal again by nightfall. The rate roads have faced the return to work in
coal allied industries. Coal-carrying roads have recalled thousands of furloughed
furloughed furloughed employees and the big steel firms are acting a bit more
slowly. One steel firm says it will be three weeks before full production is
resumed, and it will take at least a week to replenish the coal supplies. The
coal miners in Wyoming and Utah reported back in the pits today, but Montana
and Colorado, the men aren't expected to be back to work before tomorrow. Miners
in all four states seem to be very happy about the new contract sign, but the
United Mine Workers Union. And Wall Street reflected the end of the coal strike
today with the small games on many leading stocks. At one point in today's
trading the demand for railroad and industrial shares lifted the general price level to its
highest point in more than three years. Now that the coal strike has been settled, why President
Truman has decided to take another floor to vacation. The White House announced today that
the president will leave Washington next Sunday morning for a three week stay in his favorite
vacation spot, Key West Florida. The president this time will make the trip by water aboard the
presidential yacht Williamsburg. The yacht will tie up the pier near the little White House in the
the Key West and will be used to provide accommodations for the overflow of the White House personnel.
A report out of France says that the foreign ministers of the big three nations, Britain,
France and the United States will meet in London next month.
The report comes from one of the ministers, French foreign minister Robert Truman.
He says the big three diplomats will discuss a possible meeting with Russia and sources
that the French foreign office say that the date of the meeting has been set for April
the 12th, the same date foreign ministers of the 12 Atlantic PAC nations meet for discussion
of mutual defense.
In Washington, however, the State Department says that no date has been set for the big
three meeting.
The French premier's announcement said that the big three also will discuss the Far East,
including Japan, relations with communist China, aid to the Southeast Asia and Germany.
When King George opened the British Parliament today, he made no mention of one of the most
talked about subjects in the British Isles.
And that's the nationalization of the steel and the iron industries.
Now Prime Minister Atley says we should not take that omission as a sign of the labor
governments backing down.
Atley said that the laborites have not the slightest intention of dropping the nationalization
of steel in iron.
Said he, the statute is on the statute books and our purposes to give effect to the acts
passed by parliament.
The nationalization law is slated to take effect the first of October with the actual take-over
due in January.
The Atley Statement was a challenge to Winston Churchill's conservatives to fight off the
issue of nationalization in spite of a labor party's shaky majority in parliament.
Britain's Prime Minister Atley also had something to say of the talk about Britain's security
services in connection with the case of scientist Claus Sputje.
Atley said there has been a great deal of loose talk suggesting inefficiency on the part
of the security service.
He said he takes full responsibility for the efficiency of the security service, and
entirely denied all charges.
The British government today issued a white paper on defense.
It discloses that Great Britain will add $56 million to a defense budget this year, but
at the same time will reduce the personnel of her armed forces.
The total British defense budget amounts to about $2 billion, and that compares rather
feebley with the $3.5 billion President Truman has asked for this country.
Returns from Sunday's election in Greece put a leftist party, the National Progressive
Union, in the lead.
The party holds more than 12,000 votes over the nearest conservative opposition.
The heavy leftist vote in Greece is proving a surprise to politicians.
Conservative opponents are saying that the parties of the left attracted considerable
communist support, and the communists were outlawed in the Sunday election.
The start market gave up an early advance today and finished about even with previous levels.
The initial rise attributed to optimism over the resumption of the soft coal mining
had carried the average price level to its best in more than three years.
Profit taking, chipped away the games however, sales totaled 1,470,000 shares in all 1113
issues were traded of these 410 advance 401 declined and 302 remained unchanged.
The Associated Press 60 stock average was unchanged at 74.2.
The bond market at New York was higher, and cotton futures at New York closed 50 cents
a bail higher to 35 cents lower.
At Chicago, wheat closed one and a quarter to two cents higher, corn was one eighth to
a one cent higher, oats were one eighth to a quarter higher, rive was a half lower
to three quarters higher, soy beans were a quarter to one and a quarter higher, and in
the livestock market at Chicago, the hog prices were steady to 25 cents higher, with the top
at 1750 and cattle were steady to 50 cents higher with the choice absent.
The State Board of Examiners tomorrow will open bids for construction of the Montana
Veterans and Pioneers Memorial Building.
The building has been planned for several years.
There is now $725,000 available for construction, and it will be built in Helena.
A great-falls woman Mrs. William Madison is the new president of the Montana Augustana
A Lutheran Church Women's Missionary Society.
She was elected at the annual meeting yesterday in Helena.
It was held in conjunction with a planning conference
for the Luther League summer camp.
Other officers included second vice president,
Mrs. Ralph Linquist, of Great Falls.
And for those of you who are driving,
the highway department says that chains are needed
on MacDonald Pass near Helena
and on some roads leading out of view.
Highway 87 was plugged by drifts from Great Falls to Belt,
We're a 53 mile per hour gale roared in with snow and sleet early today.
Some of the roads are dangerously slippery in spots.
A British decision to cut imports of American oil
in order to save dollars seems to have backpired.
The Economic Cooperation Administration, ECA, says it's cutting off aid from the British
oil industry.
And the aid for British oilmen will stay cut off.
The ECA said that until that oil matter is ironed out to the American satisfaction.
And ECA spokesman says that it's true that the British need to conserve dollars,
but he said curtailment of oil imports is likely to harm world recovery.
The Chinese nationalists are still sitting tight in the United Nations,
and the Russians are still walking on. It happened again today.
The executive board of the International Children's Emergency Fund convened.
The nationalists showed up, sold in the Russians.
The Russians took one look at the nationalists, and then they went with the satellite delegations
trailing behind them, and that's all of the news as of now.
